verses.commentary: Jesus returns to Capernaum, his ministry headquarters. Word spreads: he had come home. Fame precedes him. The house is already the center of kingdom activity.
Overflow crowds. No room, not even at the door. Jesus preaches the word (logos, λόγος). Healing draws crowds, but teaching is the core work.
Four men carry a paralyzed friend. Faith expressed through action. They bring their need to Jesus.
Desperate faith breaks through barriers. They tear through the roof, lowering the paralytic to Jesus. Remarkable determination. True friends remove obstacles between need and Jesus.

Jesus sees their faith (plural). Corporate faith matters. But his first word is unexpected: Son, your sins are forgiven. The man came for healing; Jesus addresses the deeper problem. Sin precedes sickness in the priority of redemption.
Teachers of the law sit there, observing, judging. Silent critics in the house. Religion evaluates while the kingdom acts.

Their unspoken charge: blasphemy. Who can forgive sins but God alone? Theologically correct. But they miss the obvious conclusion: if Jesus forgives sins, he is God. They have the premise but reject the implication.
Jesus knows their thoughts. Divine knowledge. He perceives hearts. Nothing hidden from him. He brings their secret reasoning into the open.
A brilliant trap. Which is easier? To claim forgiveness (invisible, unprovable) or command healing (visible, testable)? Forgiveness requires divine authority; healing demonstrates it.

The Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins. First use of this title in Mark. From Daniel 7:13–14, the heavenly figure who receives dominion. Jesus claims divine prerogative while using a circumlocution. Authority to forgive is the central claim.
Get up, take your mat, go home. Simple commands. The visible miracle validates the invisible pronouncement.

Immediate obedience from lifeless limbs. The crowd is amazed and praises God. They recognize divine action even if they miss Jesus' identity. We have never seen anything like this. Unprecedented power.
Back to the lake. Crowds come again. Jesus teaches. The rhythm continues: teaching, crowds, ministry.

Levi (Matthew) sits at the tax collector's booth. A collaborator with Rome, a traitor to Israel. Jesus calls him. Follow me. The same call given to fishermen now given to a despised sinner. Levi rises and follows. Radical grace, radical response.

A banquet at Levi's house. Tax collectors and sinners eating with Jesus and disciples. Table fellowship signals acceptance. In Jewish culture, sharing a meal means sharing life. Jesus scandalizes by dining with the unclean.
Pharisees complain to the disciples. Indirect attack. Why does he eat with tax collectors and sinners? The company Jesus keeps reveals his mission.

Jesus overhears and responds. The sick need a doctor, not the healthy. I came to call sinners, not the righteous. Mission statement. His presence with sinners is not compromise but purpose. The righteous (self-identified) exclude themselves; sinners (self-aware) find welcome.
New criticism: your disciples don't fast. John's disciples fast, Pharisees fast, but Jesus' disciples feast. Piety demands visible disciplines. Why the exception?

Wedding guests don't fast while the bridegroom is present. Jesus identifies himself as the bridegroom. In the Old Testament, God is Israel's husband (Hosea 2:16, Isaiah 54:5). Jesus claims the role. His presence changes everything. Fasting signals absence and longing; his presence brings joy.
But the bridegroom will be taken away. First hint of the passion. A violent removal. Then they will fast. The cross casts its shadow even amid celebration.
No one patches old cloth with new. The new will tear the old. Jesus is not here to repair the old system. New covenant does not patch old covenant; it fulfills and replaces.

New wine bursts old wineskins. Both are lost. The new reality of the kingdom cannot be contained in old structures. Jesus demands new forms for new life. The age of the Spirit requires different vessels.
Sabbath controversy begins. Disciples pick grain while walking through fields. Technically permissible (Deuteronomy 23:25), but Pharisees deemed it harvesting, thus work, thus Sabbath violation.
Pharisees accuse: unlawful on the Sabbath. They police boundaries. Religion becomes about rules, not relationship.
Jesus appeals to Scripture. David, the anointed king, ate consecrated bread (1 Samuel 21:1–6). Necessity overrode ceremony. Human need supersedes ritual restriction.
David gave the bread to his companions. The high priest permitted it. Precedent established: mercy triumphs over sacrifice. The law serves life, not life the law.

The Sabbath was made for man, not man for the Sabbath. Revolutionary principle. God gave the Sabbath as gift, not burden. Rest serves humanity; humanity does not serve rest. Purpose determines practice.

The Son of Man is Lord even of the Sabbath. Second Son of Man saying. Jesus claims authority over the Sabbath, authority to interpret and even supersede it. Only the Creator of the Sabbath can make this claim. The chapter ends with escalating conflict and escalating claims. Forgiveness, feasting, and Sabbath rest all find their meaning in Jesus.
